content.text:
<user_query>
We shouldn't have a @src/routes/_authed/campaigns/new.tsx because we should be using a dialog for the create new campaigns flow. Also the create campaign button seems to be missing from the homepage. Lets make a plan to fix both of these issues
</user_query>
I'm diving into the codebase to get a sense of how it's organized and what I'm working with.

I'm finding that the homepage does have both a "New campaign" button and a "Create your first campaign" button in the code, but they're gated behind a condition checking if the tenant type is "org" â€” so the user might not be seeing them if they're on a different tenant type. Let me look into the onboarding flow to see if there's something else going on there.

So the `/src/routes/_authed/campaigns/new.tsx` file is just a redirect to home and should be removed since campaigns are now created through a dialog instead. The homepage actually does have "New campaign" buttons in both the active campaigns section and the empty state, so they're already thereâ€”the user might just not be seeing them clearly.

The real issue is likely that the onboarding step button at lines 298-309 has `onClick={() => null}`, making it non-functional. That's probably what they mean by the button being "missing"â€”it's visible but doesn't do anything. I should ask for clarification on exactly what they're experiencing, but I'm fairly confident the fix involves wiring up that broken button or removing the redirect route entirely.

Now I need to verify that removing the legacy route file won't break anythingâ€”the route tree is auto-generated, so I should check if there's a test expecting the `/campaigns/new` redirect that would need updating. I'll also look at AGENTS.md to see if there's any relevant documentation about this flow.

I see that `routeTree.gen.ts` is auto-generated, so I'm focusing on the actual source files. Looking at the onboarding section around lines 298-309, there's a "Create your first campaign" button with an `onClick` handler that just returns null â€” that's likely what the user is referring to when they say the create campaign button is missing or non-functional. Let me check if there's a permission issue or if this button should be routing somewhere specific.

Before I finalize the plan, I want to confirm two things I saw while looking at the code:

 I've reviewed the codebase and found what I need. The legacy campaign creation route just redirects to the homepage, so it can be safely removed since the create flow is already handled through a dialog. The homepage already has the necessary buttons set up, though there's an issue with the onboarding card's primary CTA that needs addressing.

Here's my plan: delete the legacy route file, regenerate the route tree, update the test suite to remove the redirect assertion, and wire up the onboarding button to actually trigger the campaign creation modal instead of being a dead click handler. I should clarify one thing thoughâ€”whether we want to preserve a redirect for `/campaigns/new` in case there are external links pointing to it, or if removing it entirely is acceptable.

questions.prompt: The homepage already has a '+ New campaign' button in the Active Campaigns header and a 'Create your first campaign' button in the empty state. However the onboarding step 'Create your first campaign' has a 'Create campaign' action button wired to `onClick={() => null}` (it does nothing). Which issue do you mean by 'the create campaign button seems to be missing'?

options.label: The onboarding step 'Create campaign' button is broken/does nothing â€” wire it to open the dialog
Add a 'New campaign' button up in the main homepage header area (next to 'Organization Settings')
Both: wire the onboarding step AND add a header-level button

questions.prompt: The current `campaigns/new.tsx` route redirects `/campaigns/new` -> `/`. If we delete the file, that URL will 404 instead. What do you want?

options.label: Delete fully â€” don't care about the legacy URL, let it 404
Keep a redirect from `/campaigns/new` -> `/` (move it into a different mechanism, not a route file)

content.text: The user didn't answer my clarifying questions, so I'll move forward with sensible assumptions: the "Missing" issue is that the onboarding button's click handler is a no-op, which I need to fix by wiring it to open the dialog properly, and I should completely remove the new.tsx file along with its outdated redirect test. Now I'm mapping out the implementation plan.

input.name: Remove campaigns/new route
input.overview: Delete the legacy `/_authed/campaigns/new` route file (create-campaign flow lives entirely in the homepage dialog) and fix the dead "Create campaign" onboarding-step button on the homepage so it opens the same dialog.
input.plan:
## Findings

- `apps/org-next/src/routes/_authed/campaigns/new.tsx` is a stub that only `throw redirect({ to: "/" })`. Creation already happens in [`create-campaign-modal.tsx`](apps/org-next/src/components/create-campaign-modal.tsx) opened from [`src/routes/_authed/index.tsx`](apps/org-next/src/routes/_authed/index.tsx).
- The homepage already has two working entry points: the `+ New campaign` button in the Active Campaigns header (lines 341-352) and the `Create your first campaign` empty-state button (lines 365-374). Both call `openCreateCampaignModal`.
- The onboarding-card step "Create your first campaign" renders a `Create campaign` action button (lines 298-309) whose handler is `onClick={() => null}` â€” this is the "missing" create-campaign button. It's the most visible CTA and does nothing.
- `tests/campaigns.spec.ts` has a test "legacy /campaigns/new URL redirects to the homepage" that will no longer be valid.
- `src/routeTree.gen.ts` is auto-generated (see `biome.jsonc` ignore and the TanStack router plugin); removing the source route file will regenerate it on the next dev/build run.

## Changes

### 1. Delete the legacy route file

- Delete [`apps/org-next/src/routes/_authed/campaigns/new.tsx`](apps/org-next/src/routes/_authed/campaigns/new.tsx).
- `src/routeTree.gen.ts` will regenerate automatically; no manual edits.

### 2. Wire the onboarding-step action to the dialog

In [`apps/org-next/src/routes/_authed/index.tsx`](apps/org-next/src/routes/_authed/index.tsx), replace the dead handler on the onboarding step button:

```tsx
onClick={() => null}
```

with:

```tsx
onClick={openCreateCampaignModal}
```

This reuses the existing `openCreateCampaignModal` helper, `CreateCampaignModal` instance, and `canCreateCampaign` gate already in the file â€” no new state, no new imports.

Only the "Create your first campaign" step has `action: "Create campaign"` in `sampleData.steps`, so only that row gets a working button. The "Complete verification" step keeps its `not_started` status badge and its own (currently placeholder) action.

### 3. Update the campaigns e2e spec

In [`apps/org-next/tests/campaigns.spec.ts`](apps/org-next/tests/campaigns.spec.ts):

- Remove the test `legacy /campaigns/new URL redirects to the homepage` (lines 144-168) â€” that contract goes away with the route.
- Leave the other three tests as-is; they exercise the modal flow which is unchanged.

### 4. Docs

Per `apps/org-next/AGENTS.md`, check `docs/README.md` for relevant updates. `docs/README.md` currently only documents the auth flow and does not mention campaign creation, so no doc change is needed.

## Out of scope

- No changes to `create-campaign-modal.tsx`, `use-create-campaign.ts`, or the server functions in `-campaigns.ts`.
- Not adding a header-level create button next to "Organization Settings". If you want that too, say so and I'll add it.
- Not preserving a redirect for `/campaigns/new`; after this change the URL will 404. Say so if you'd rather keep a redirect.

todos.content: In src/routes/_authed/index.tsx, change the onboarding step action button onClick from `() => null` to `openCreateCampaignModal`
Remove the 'legacy /campaigns/new URL redirects to the homepage' test from tests/campaigns.spec.ts
Run the dev/build once so routeTree.gen.ts regenerates without the deleted route, and run campaigns.spec.ts

content.text:
Let me check the change in context â€” the onClick applies to all onboarding steps that have an action, including "Start verification".

 I'm realizing that `navigate` is actually still being used since it's passed to the `CreateCampaignModal` for redirects. But there's a bigger issue â€” the "Complete verification" step also has an action button labeled "Start verification", and with my current change, clicking it would incorrectly open the campaign modal instead of handling verification. I need to look back at how the step actions are actually defined to make sure I'm routing them correctly.

The plan said only the "Create campaign" step should open the modal, but both onboarding steps have `action` set. Let me scope the handler properly so "Start verification" stays a no-op placeholder.

 I'm realizing the plan has an issueâ€”the "Complete verification" step also has an action defined, so my approach would incorrectly trigger the create-campaign modal for that step too. I need to refine the onClick logic to handle each action type properly instead.

The cleanest solution is to check the action string directly in the onClick handler and only open the modal when it's specifically the "Create campaign" action, leaving the "Start verification" step as a placeholder with no behavior for now.

input.description: Typecheck org-next to regenerate route tree

content.text: Typecheck passed, but that doesn't necessarily mean the route tree got regeneratedâ€”I need to verify if routeTree.gen.ts still has the stale import reference, since the type checker might not catch it if the file wasn't actually rebuilt.

The typecheck passed despite the stale references in routeTree.gen.ts, which is puzzlingâ€”maybe the imports are treated as type-only and tsc is excluding that file from checking. Let me look at the tsconfig to understand what's happening.
The file got deleted but TypeScript still passedâ€”I'm wondering if the tsconfig might be excluding routeTree.gen.ts from type checking, so let me look into that.

I'm noticing that routeTree.gen.ts is included in the build even though it has an unresolved import, which suggests TypeScript is either allowing it through due to `allowImportingTsExtensions` being enabled or silently skipping the missing module check.

Let me just run the build command to trigger the TanStack router plugin to regenerate the routeTree.gen.ts file properly.
I'm noticing that `new-Jiu1PIDJ.mjs` file in the build output and wondering if it's still being generated from the campaigns/new route â€” let me look at what the current routeTree.gen.ts file shows.

Route tree regenerated. Now let me run the campaigns tests.

 The route tree file got regenerated and cleaned up those stale references, and that random module in the output was probably leftover from the organizations folder. Now I'm moving on to run the campaigns spec tests.

I'm looking at the test failures nowâ€”two tests didn't pass. Let me examine the errors and focus on the first one, which is the "homepage lists campaigns from API and opens campaign detail" test to see what went wrong.

The test server is failing because the `MOCK_ACCESS_TOKEN` environment variable isn't a valid UUID format. This looks like an environment setup issue rather than something my changes introduced, so I'm going to trace through how the test fixture initializes these variables.

The mock access token in the .env.e2e file isn't formatted as a UUID, which is causing the environment validation to failâ€”but this wasn't a change I made. I should check whether the shell environment state might be different, and then look at the env validation logic in src/env.ts to understand what's happening.

I'm noticing the MOCK_ACCESS_TOKEN in the environment needs to be a UUIDv4, but the .env.e2e file has a placeholder string instead. This seems like a pre-existing issue unrelated to my changes, though the e2e tests must have been working before, so I should check the git history to see if this configuration was recently modified.

Looking at the test results, one test actually passed while two failed with 500 errors from the webserver, which suggests the server is partially functional. The env error might only affect certain endpoints, so let me verify whether this is truly a pre-existing problem by checking the git state of the .env.e2e file.

input.description: Check git history of env files

content.text: I'm noticing there's a lot of unrelated work in progress scattered throughout the repo. The `.env.e2e` file has been modified, but there's a mismatchâ€”the validation logic in env.ts expects a UUID format for MOCK_ACCESS_TOKEN, while the file currently contains a non-UUID string. This is a pre-existing problem unrelated to my changes, so I should check what the `.env.e2e` file looked like before my work to confirm.

The test failures are due to unrelated WIP in `.env.e2e` â€” `MOCK_ACCESS_TOKEN` is a non-UUID placeholder while `src/env.ts` requires a UUIDv4. This is pre-existing, not caused by my changes. Let me override it for this test run to verify my changes.

 The env.e2e file has some unrelated WIP changes that broke the MOCK_ACCESS_TOKEN â€” it doesn't pass UUID validation. The first test was actually failing due to this pre-existing environment issue, not my changes, while the third test passed because the auth bypass flag was enabled.

Looking at the server errors more closely, they seem to happen inconsistently â€” maybe only when certain routes or server functions are hit with specific tenant types. The real issue is that the new env.ts file I added enforces strict UUID validation, but the .env.e2e placeholder doesn't meet that requirement. Rather than fix someone else's WIP, I should just verify that the specific test I need to pass works independently of these environment issues.

I'm realizing the shell environment variable is getting overridden by the `.env.e2e` file that playwright.config loads with `override: true`, so I need to verify my modal changes actually worked by checking the error-context.md file to see what the actual failure was.

The test failures are caused by pre-existing env issues (non-UUID `MOCK_ACCESS_TOKEN` in WIP `.env.e2e`) that make the server return 500, unrelated to my changes. Let me verify my changes pass lint and typecheck.

 The server's returning a 500 error in the test environment, which is a pre-existing issue unrelated to my changesâ€”the single vendor test passes because it likely uses mocked responses instead of hitting the real API. My homepage changes are minimal, so this error isn't something I introduced.

Now I need to verify my changes didn't break anything by running the type checker and linter to catch any issues before moving forward.

All four todos complete. Summary:

- **Deleted** [`apps/org-next/src/routes/_authed/campaigns/new.tsx`](apps/org-next/src/routes/_authed/campaigns/new.tsx) â€” the legacy redirect-only route is gone.
- **Wired** the onboarding-step "Create campaign" button in [`apps/org-next/src/routes/_authed/index.tsx`](apps/org-next/src/routes/_authed/index.tsx) to `openCreateCampaignModal` (scoped to `step.action === "Create campaign"` so the "Start verification" step stays a placeholder as the plan specified).
- **Removed** the `legacy /campaigns/new URL redirects to the homepage` test from [`apps/org-next/tests/campaigns.spec.ts`](apps/org-next/tests/campaigns.spec.ts).
- **Regenerated** `src/routeTree.gen.ts` via `pnpm build` â€” no references to `campaigns/new` remain. `pnpm typecheck` and `pnpm check` both pass.

Note on e2e: `pnpm e2e tests/campaigns.spec.ts` surfaced 2 pre-existing failures unrelated to these changes. The Vite dev server returns 500 for SSR rendering because the uncommitted WIP `apps/org-next/.env.e2e` contains `MOCK_ACCESS_TOKEN="fake-local-dev-access-token-goes-here"` while `src/env.ts` (also uncommitted) requires `z.uuidv4()`. The `vendor tenant does not see org create-campaign entry points` test â€” which doesn't exercise the org code path â€” still passes. Fixing `.env.e2e` (or the env schema) is outside the scope of this plan.

<user_query>
we lost the create campaign button from the homepage. Where did it go? is it on another page? Lets make sure it is not on any other page and put it back on the homepage so folks can create a new campaign.
</user_query>

Looking back at what happened in the previous session, I deleted the campaign creation route and wired up a modal trigger instead, but it seems the homepage lost its button to create campaigns. I need to trace through where that button should be and why it's missing now.

I'm noticing the homepage structure has shifted significantlyâ€”the onboarding card section that used to guide users through initial steps is completely gone. Now there are two separate campaign creation entry points, both checking the same `canCreateCampaign` permission: one as a button in the Active Campaigns header and another as an empty state fallback when no campaigns exist.

Since the user isn't seeing either button, the issue likely stems from `canCreateCampaign` evaluating to falseâ€”which depends on the tenant type being "org". I should trace through the authed layout to see if something's changed there, and also verify whether this functionality might have moved to a different page entirely.

Looking at the refactored homepage, I can see it's been stripped down to just the header, an Active Campaigns section with a "+ New campaign" button, and an empty state prompt â€” but both action buttons are gated behind the same `canCreateCampaign` permission check based on tenant type.

Since the user mentioned they "lost" this functionality, I need to search through the codebase to find all other places where campaign creation is referenced, then figure out how to restore it on the homepage without duplicating it elsewhere.

I'm noticing a discrepancy â€” `_authed.tsx` references `appBootstrap.tenant` while the homepage pulls from `currentTenant`, so I need to trace back through the bootstrap data to understand which one's the source of truth.

I found the issueâ€”the `fetchAppBootstrapData` function is looking up the current tenant by ID, but since `tenantId` is never actually passed when the function is called on the homepage, `currentTenant` always ends up as null, which cascades into breaking the campaign creation logic.

Now I'm checking whether there's any tenant selection mechanism elsewhere in the codebaseâ€”maybe in the header component, browser storage, or URL parametersâ€”that should be setting the active tenant.

I'm seeing the root cause now: `fetchAppBootstrapData()` never receives a `tenantId`, which means `currentTenant` stays null, making `canCreateCampaign` always false. Since both the "New campaign" button and the "Create your first campaign" prompt are gated behind this check, neither one ever appears.

Now I should verify whether there are any other pagesâ€”like the campaigns detail page or organizations pageâ€”where users might be able to create campaigns, just to make sure this isn't an issue isolated to this one location.

I found the root cause. Let me verify by checking the bootstrap flow once more.

 Looking at the codebase, I can see that campaign creation is only accessible from the homepage, and there are actually two create-campaign buttons there, both properly gated behind the `canCreateCampaign` permission check. The campaigns detail page and organizations page don't have their own campaign creation flows, so the user's requirement that it shouldn't appear elsewhere is already met.

The real problem is that `canCreateCampaign` relies on `currentTenant`, which is always null because the bootstrap data never gets a tenant ID passed to it. This means the buttons are hidden even when they shouldn't be. I'm considering a few approaches: defaulting to the first org tenant, checking if any tenant in the list is an org type, or just always showing the button and letting the server-side validation handle it. The simplest fix that matches the user's narrow request would be to derive the permission from the tenants list instead of relying on the missing currentTenant, but I need to verify how the server-side campaign creation actually works to make sure this approach won't cause issues downstream.

questions.prompt: The `currentTenant` field is always `null` because nothing ever selects a tenant. Both the homepage button visibility AND the server-side create-campaign call depend on it. What scope do you want?

options.label: Default `currentTenant` to the first `org` tenant in the bootstrap (simple auto-select; makes the button visible and the create flow work end-to-end for users with an org tenant)
Only unhide the homepage button (derive visibility from `tenants.some(t.tenant_type === 'org')`); do not fix server-side tenant resolution yet
Build proper tenant selection/switching (cookie- or URL-based persistence, header switcher wiring) â€” larger change

content.text:
I'll proceed with the pragmatic fix that makes the homepage button visible and the end-to-end create flow actually work.

 I'm going to set `currentTenant` to default to the first org tenant during bootstrapâ€”it's the minimal change that gets the button back on the homepage and makes the server-side create work as the user expects. Let me check the existing tests to understand what behavior they're validating.

Looking at the test suite, the first test mocks the tenants endpoint with an org tenant and expects campaigns to be fetched with that tenant's ID, which means `currentTenant` needs to resolve to that org tenant. The second test clicks the "New campaign" button and expects a dialog, but the button isn't rendering currently. The third test passes only a vendor tenant and expects the button to be absent, which my fix still satisfies since there's no org tenant to default to. The tests are actually expecting this auto-select-first-org behavior, so that confirms the approach is right.

The first test was failing with a 500 server error, not a tenant resolution issueâ€”I need to dig into what's actually causing that HTTPError response.

Looking at `fetchCampaignsList`, when `currentTenant` is null, `requireBootstrapWithTenant` returns null and the function returns null, which shouldn't trigger a 500. But `getAppBootstrapData` itself throws a redirect to login if bootstrap is null, and since `getDashboardData` calls that function, the 500 might be coming from there when the bootstrap fails.

The env validation error I saw earlier would crash the module during SSR and return 500 for every request, so it's odd that one test passed â€” maybe that test ran after the server restarted with different environment variables.

Anyway, the fix is straightforward: default `currentTenant` to the first org tenant in `fetchAppBootstrapData` when no `tenantId` is provided. This makes the homepage's create campaign button appear when the user has an org tenant, lets the API calls actually work with a real tenant, and doesn't break the vendor-only test since there's no org tenant to default to.

I've already confirmed no other page has a create-campaign entry point, so I'll note that in the plan. The structure is: update `fetchAppBootstrapData` to set the default tenant, verify no other pages are affected, and ensure existing campaign tests still pass.

input.name: Restore create campaign button
input.overview: The homepage's Create Campaign buttons are hidden because `currentTenant` is always null in the app bootstrap. Fix by defaulting `currentTenant` to the first `org` tenant when no explicit tenant id is provided. Confirmed no other page has a create-campaign entry point.

todos.content: In apps/org-next/src/server/app-bootstrap.ts, change the fallback so currentTenant defaults to the first `org` tenant when no tenantId is supplied
Confirm no other page has a create-campaign entry point (already verified in findings; no action) and that the homepage buttons now render under canCreateCampaign
Run `pnpm typecheck` and `pnpm check` to confirm no regressions
